coredata.dc:title: Bi4O5Br2 ultrasmall nanosheets in situ strong coupling to MWCNT and improved photocatalytic activity for tetracycline hydrochloride degradation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Molecular Catalysis A: Chemical
coredata.dc:creator: Di, Jun
Ji, Mengxia
Xia, Jiexiang
Li, Xiaowei
Fan, Wenmin
Zhang, Qi
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description:
Multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT)/Bi4O5Br2 materials have been prepared with the Bi4O5Br2 ultrasmall nanosheets in situ strong coupling to MWCNT via the ionic liquid (IL) assisted solvothermal method. The formation mechanism was based on the original formation of strong coupling between the IL and MWCNT, and then the tight junctions between Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT can be established as the bromine ions in the IL react with bismuth source to in situ produce Bi4O5Br2 during solvothermal process. The structures, morphologies, surface features, optical properties, electronic structures and photocatalytic properties were investigated in detail. The MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials exhibited increased photocatalytic activity than pure Bi4O5Br2 for the degradation of antibiotic agent tetracycline hydrochloride and rhodamine B. Meanwhile, the 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials displayed the highest activity. The hole and O2
− were determined to be the main active species during the photodegradation process under visible light irradiation by the electron spin resonance, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy valence band spectra and active species trapping experiments. After the introduction of MWCNT, the molecular oxygen activation capacity of Bi4O5Br2 greatly improved, which was believed to be responsible for the increased photocatalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi4O5Br2
MWCNT
Photocatalytic
Ionic liquid
Strong coupling

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Molecular Catalysis A: Chemical

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns of as-prepared MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 samples.
The XPS spectra of the pure Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials. (a) Full scan; (b) Bi 4f; (c) O 1s; (d) Br 4f; (e) C 1s.
Raman spectra of the pure Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials with different MWCNT content.
Typical TEM images of the sample of (a–c) 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials and (d) pure Bi4O5Br2.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the as-prepared MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials.
Nitrogen absorptionedesorption isotherms of (a) pure Bi4O5Br2, (b) 0.05wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2, (c) 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 and (d) 0.2wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presence of pure Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 samples under visible light irradiation; (b) Photocatalytic degradation of TC in the presence of pure Bi4O5Br2 and 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 samples under visible light irradiation; (c) Kinetic fit for the degradation of RhB with the pure Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials.
The photocurrent responses of pure Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials in 0.1M PBS electrolyte.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy plots of pure Bi4O5Br2 and 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials in the dark.
PL spectra of pure Bi4O5Br2 and 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials, with excitation wavelength of 360nm.

DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra recorded with (a, b) pure Bi4O5Br2 materials and (c, d) 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst in (a, c) methanol dispersion (for DMPO-O2
−) and (b, d) aqueous dispersion (for DMPO-OH) under visible light irradiation.
(a) Valence-band XPS spectra of the Bi4O5Br2 and 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials; (b) the (αEphoton) 1/2 vs. Ephoton curves of the as-prepared pure Bi4O5Br2.
Trapping experiment of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials under visible light irradiation.
Schematic of the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges in the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials combined with the possible reaction mechanism of photocatalytic procedure.
Pseudo-first-order rate constant for RhB photocatalytic oxidation under different photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Bi4O5Br2 ultrasmall nanosheets in situ strong coupling to MWCNT and improved photocatalytic activity for tetracycline hydrochloride degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT)/Bi4O5Br2 materials have been prepared with the Bi4O5Br2 ultrasmall nanosheets in situ strong coupling to MWCNT via the ionic liquid (IL) assisted solvothermal method. The formation mechanism was based on the original formation of strong coupling between the IL and MWCNT, and then the tight junctions between Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT can be established as the bromine ions in the IL react with bismuth source to in situ produce Bi4O5Br2 during solvothermal process. The structures, morphologies, surface features, optical properties, electronic structures and photocatalytic properties were investigated in detail. The MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials exhibited increased photocatalytic activity than pure Bi4O5Br2 for the degradation of antibiotic agent tetracycline hydrochloride and rhodamine B. Meanwhile, the 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials displayed the highest activity. The hole and O2
− were determined to be the main active species during the photodegradation process under visible light irradiation by the electron spin resonance, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy valence band spectra and active species trapping experiments. After the introduction of MWCNT, the molecular oxygen activation capacity of Bi4O5Br2 greatly improved, which was believed to be responsible for the increased photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of graphene, the two-dimensional (2D) layered materials such as borophenes, h-BN, MoS2, g-C3N4, and CoSe2 have aroused more and more attentions [1–5]. Thanks to their fascinating properties, they give rise to promise and potential applications ranging from electronics to catalysis [6]. Recent researches demonstrated that the 2D layered semiconductor materials with a suitable band gap may also bring new opportunities for photocatalysis [7–9].

As a new family of promising layered materials, the bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, X=Cl, Br, and I), have attracted substantial research interest owning to their remarkable photocatalytic activities [10–16]. The layered structure of BiOX enabled the generation of internal static electric fields along the [001] orientation, with the polarization of the related atoms and orbitals in BiOX [17,18]. Especially, the BiOBr has being widely concerned since the superior visible light photocatalytic activity [19]. To further increase the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr, several strategies have been employed to regulate the BiOBr materials, such as specific crystal facets exposure [20,21], doping [22], surface functionalization [23–25], and semiconductor hybridization [26–31]. Among which the dehalogenation strategy has been demonstrated to be able to optimize the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr. Some bismuth oxybromides with different halogen contents have been controlled synthesized, such as Bi12O17Br2 [32,33], Bi5O7Br [34], Bi3O4Br[35,36] and Bi24O31Br10 [37]. Very recently, the Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets have been first controlled prepared by our group [38]. The Bi4O5Br2 displayed negative conduction band position and wider valence band than BiOBr, which facilitated the charge carrier migration among the material interior and was believed to be responsible for the improved photocatalytic activity. However, the photocatalytic activity of pure Bi4O5Br2 was still limited by reason of the high surface charge carrier recombination rate. Considering the superficial charge carriers was slowness to take part in the interface redox reactions, it was desirable to increase the surface separating efficiency of charge carriers apart from the bulk separating efficiency.

Carbon nanotubes (CNT), have been demonstrated to produce beneficial effects on the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts, thanks to its outstanding electron transfer ability. It has been employed in applications ranging from pollutant removal, disinfection of bacteria, H2 generation, to selective organic synthesis [39–41]. Several photocatalytic systems based on the CNT have been studied and the increased activity has been obtained, such as TiO2 [40], ZnO [41], CdS [42,43], C3N4 [44], Bi2WO6 [45], Ag3PO4 [46], and Ag/AgX [47]. However, seldom specialists have dedicated to the increased coupling interaction between the semiconductor and CNT. The simple integration of photocatalysts with CNT was not able to build a maximum interfacial contact and strong-coupling interaction between the semiconductor and CNT. Considering the significance of carriers transferring between the semiconductor and CNT for the improved photocatalytic activity, it was urgent and imperative to build their interface with strong coupling interaction, so as to utilize the excellent electron conductivity of CNT sufficiently.

Recent studies have demonstrated that the ionic liquid (IL) played important role for the controlled preparation of different inorganic functional nanomaterials by reason of its different functions such as solvent, reactant, template, capping agent, dispersing agent, and so on [48,49]. In this system, the reactive IL 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([C16mim]Br) was employed to assist prepare multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) modified Bi4O5Br2 materials. As the important noncovalent interactions in the IL system, the hydrogen bond and coulomb force of IL could act on the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of MWCNT, thereby the strong coupling between MWCNT and IL was constructed [50,51]. Subsequently, the bromine ions in the IL [C16mim]Br could react with bismuth nitrate to generate Bi4O5Br2 nanocrystal in situ and give rise to the strong coupling of MWCNT and Bi4O5Br2 during the solvothermal process.

Herein, MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 hybrid materials have been controlled prepared via the reactive IL assisted solvothermal process. The IL [C16mim]Br was utilized to supply the Br source as well as worked as a bridge to intimately integrate MWCNT and Bi4O5Br2 materials. As a result, the excellent electron conductivity of MWCNT can be used sufficiently. The photocatalytic activity of MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials was evaluated by the degradation of antibiotic agent tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. The structure-activity relationship was discussed in details and some new insight regarding the effect of MWCNT for the increased photocatalytic activity was proposed. The photocatalytic mechanism was presented according to the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy valence band spectra, active species trapping experiments and electron spin resonance analysis.

All the reagents were of analytical purity and were used as received. The ionic liquid [C16mim]Br (1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide) (99%) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co. Ltd. The MWCNT (MWCNT-COOH, purity>95%) was purchased from Chengdu Organic Chemicals Co. Ltd., Chinese Academy of Sciences.

During the synthesis process, 1mmol [C16mim]Br and certain amount of MWCNT was dispersed into 0.1mol/L mannitol solution under stirring. Then the 1mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into the above solution and the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 11.5 with 2mol/L NaOH solution. After the mixture was stirred for 30min at room temperature, the mixture was transferred into a 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 140°C for 24h. After the solvothermal reaction, the as-prepared samples were acquired by centrifuge and rinsed adequately with ethanol and distilled water as well as dried at 50°C in air. The obtained composite was denoted as X wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2, where X wt% was the initial weight ratio of MWCNT. For comparison, pure Bi4O5Br2 was obtained without the addition of MWCNT.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer) with Cu Kα radiation was employed to characterize the crystal structure of the obtained materials. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a VG MultiLab 2000 system with a monochromatic Mg Kα source operated at 20kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurements were performed on a Nexus 470 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Raman spectroscopy was conducted on a Renishaw inVia Raman System with a 532nm laser excitation source at room temperature. The morphology of the samples was determined by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-7001F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100). The optical properties of the obtained samples were analyzed by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV2450), in which BaSO4 was used as the internal reflectance standard. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas were determined using Micromeritics TriStar II 3020 Surface Area and Porosity Analyzer at 77K. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra for solid samples were investigated on a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrophotometer. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of spin-trapped oxidative radicals were acquired on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer using spin-trap reagent DMPO (Sigma Chemical Co.) in water for hydroxyl radical and methanol for superoxide radical, respectively. Photoelectrochemical measurements were performed in CHI 660B electrochemical system with a constructed three electrode system. A Pt wire was used as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl electrode was employed as the reference electrode, while the working electrode was prepared on ITO conductor glass. The electrolyte solution of photocurrent was phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (0.1molL−1, pH=7.0). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out in a 0.1M KCl solution containing 5mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4−.

The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials was evaluated by the degradation of antibiotic agent tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and rhodamine B (RhB) in solution under visible light irradiation. Visible light irradiation was obtained from a 300W Xe lamp with a 400nm cutoff filter. The distance between the light source and the Pyrex glass was approximate 10cm. The photocatalytic experiments were performed at 30°C with a circulating water system to prevent thermal catalytic effects. For the photocatalytic experiments, 20mg and 30mg of photocatalyst was totally dispersed in an RhB solution (100mL, 10mg/L) and TC solution (100mL, 20mg/L), respectively. Before irradiation, the suspensions were magnetically stirred in the dark for 30min to achieve absorption-desorption equilibrium between the samples and RhB. Aeration was performed using an air pump to ensure a constant supply of oxygen. At certain time intervals, 3mL suspension were sampled and centrifuged to remove the particles. The concentration of TC and RhB was analyzed by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2450) according to its absorbance at 356 and 553nm, respectively.

The crystallization and phase structure of the as-prepared samples was revealed by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. As shown in Fig. 1 , all of the observed peaks of the pattern can be indexed to the pure monoclinic Bi4O5Br2 phase (JCPDS no. 37-0699). With respect to the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 samples, no characteristic peak of MWCNT (about 26°) can be detected, which was assigned to the low content of MWCNT in the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials. Similar results can also be observed in literatures [45,52]. No other peak of impurities can be observed, suggesting the high purity of the samples.

To investigate the surface compositions and chemical states of the as-prepared MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed and the result is shown in Fig. 2 . The full scan results in Fig. 2a reveals that Bi, O, Br and C elements were present in the 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 sample. As shown in Fig. 2b, the spin-orbit of the Bi 4f of Bi4O5Br2 could be well distinguished as two peaks at 164.4 and 159.0eV, corresponding to the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 orbits of Bi3+, respectively. The Bi 4f peak in the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 sample showed a slight shift when compared to the Bi 4f peak in the pure Bi4O5Br2 sample. This result indicated that the surface Bi chemical environment in the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 has changed and can be referred to the presence of Bi-C interaction in the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 sample. Meanwhile, the O 1s binding energy peak centered at 529.9eV was ascribed to bridging oxygen atoms from BiOBi bonds (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2d exhibits the peak at 68.4eV, corresponding to the binding energy of Br 3d for the Bi4O5Br2 sample. From Fig. 2e, the C peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.3eV can be observed, which was come from the CC bond with the sp2 orbital, COC bond, and CO bond, respectively [53]. This result suggested that the MWCNT has been introduced to the Bi4O5Br2 materials successfully.

Raman spectroscopy has also been performed to further determine the existence of MWCNT in the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials. Fig. 3 shows the typical Raman spectra of the pure Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 with different MWCNT contents under the excitation of a 532nm laser. The bands located at 93 and 141cm−1 for pure Bi4O5Br2 materials were attributed to the A1g internal, and E1g internal BiBr stretching modes, respectively. With respect to the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials, two peaks at 1346 and 1584cm−1 can be seen, corresponding to the D-band and G-band of carbon in MWCNT, respectively [54]. With the improvement of MWCNT content, the peaks of carbon gradually increased, revealing the MWCNT has been introduced to the Bi4O5Br2 successfully. The ratio of ID/IG did not showed distinct variation with the increased MWCNT content. The Raman result was in consistent with the XPS analysis, further revealing the MWCNT has been introduced to the Bi4O5Br2 successfully.

The microscopic morphology and structure information of the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials was determined by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the MWCNT and Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets have been interlaced together and good contact between the MWCNT and the Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets can be built. It can be seen from Fig. 4b, c that the size of Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets was less than 20nm, revealing the ultrasmall Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets have been obtained and connected with MWCNT. As compared with the pure Bi4O5Br2 (Fig. 4d), the size of Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets became smaller in the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials. The introduction of MWCNT could induce the generation of smaller Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets, further revealing the existence of strong-coupling interaction between the MWCNT and Bi4O5Br2 rather than the simple mechanical integration.

The optical properties of the sample were determined by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) measurement. Fig. 5 displays the DRS of pure Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials with different weight addition ratios of MWCNT. The Bi4O5Br2 sample showed the onset absorption at 500nm. The introduction of MWCNT significantly increased the absorption in the visible light region ranging from 450 to 800nm. This can be attributed to the broad background absorption of MWCNT in the visible light region which was in consistent with the color change of the samples from yellow to gray [52].

The surface area and porosity of pure Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials with different MWCNT content have been studied via the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm. As shown in Fig. 6 , the Bi4O5Br2 and the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 samples both exhibits type IV isotherm with a typical H3 hysteresis loop characteristic of mesoporous solids [55]. The BET specific surface area of the Bi4O5Br2, 0.05wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2, 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 and 0.2wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 samples were calculated to be 33.38, 98.17, 88.21 and 92.67m2 g−1, respectively. It can be found that the introduction of MWCNT could enlarge the BET surface area of Bi4O5Br2. However, the content of MWCNT shows no significant influence on the surface area of the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 samples, suggesting the improvement of BET surface area was not resulted from the large surface area of MWCNT. This enhanced BET surface area may be ascribed to that the Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets become smaller by this in situ coupling and growth with MWNCT.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials was evaluated by the degradation of RhB under the visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 7 a, 61.8% RhB can be removed by pure Bi4O5Br2 sample by irradiation for 75min. After the MWCNT was introduced to the Bi4O5Br2, the photocatalytic activity greatly increased, which 68.4%, 83.1% and 76.0% of RhB can be degraded by the 0.05wt%, 0.1wt% and 0.2wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials, respectively. The 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 sample displayed the optimal photocatalytic activity. When a low amount of MWCNT was modified on the Bi4O5Br2, the MWCNT could effectively transfer the photo-generated electrons from Bi4O5Br2 after being illuminated under visible light due to the excellent electron transfer ability of MWCNT. However, when the MWCNT content was higher than 0.1wt%, a further increase of MWCNT content lead to a decay of the photocatalytic activity for RhB degradation. This was attributed to that the MWCNT could absorb the visible light and then shield the Bi4O5Br2 from light absorption. Thus, too much content of MWCNT could result in the decrease of photocatalytic activity. The degradation product has been determined via the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). It can be seen from Fig. S1 that the RhB was photodegraded into several intermediates such as N,N-diethyl-N′-ethylrhodamine (DER), N-ethyl-N′-ethylrhodamine (EER) and (DR). To test the stability of the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials, the 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 material was collected after photocatalytic degradation experiment of RhB. As shown in Fig. S2, the photocatalytic activity could still be maintained after four cycles with only 4.8% decrease of the degradation efficiency. To further confirm the stability of the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 material, we compare the XRD of the 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 sample before and after photocatalysis reactions (Fig. S3). The XRD patterns were carried out to determine the structure variations of the sample. The results revealed that the crystal structure of the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 material do not change after the photocatalytic degradation reaction.

Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) has been widely employed as an antibiotic agent for treating bacterial infections. Since it cannot be metabolized completely, a large portion of TC was discharged into aquatic environments as the active form pharmaceutically. The largely discharge and lack of treatment may promote the antibiotic resistance within native bacterial populations in impacted environments. Here, the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 material was employed for the removal of TC under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 7b, the blank test revealing the direct photolysis of TC was negligible. After irradiation for 30min, 41.9% of TC can be photodegraded by the pure Bi4O5Br2 material. And after the MWCNT was introduced to the Bi4O5Br2, the degradation efficiency could improve 21% within the same time. When the irradiation time extended to 120min, 86.2% of TC can be removed by the 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials.

Fig. 7c illustrates the reaction kinetics of RhB degradation by using MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials following pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model by the equation of −ln(C/C0)=kt, in which the k represent the rate constant, C0 and C were the RhB concentrations in solution at times 0 and t, respectively. The 0.1wt% MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 sample displayed the optimal rate constant (0.0249min−1) which was approximate 2 times higher than pure Bi4O5Br2 materials. This result suggested that suitable MWCNT modification can efficiently promote the photocatalytic performance of Bi4O5Br2 (Table 1 ).

The charge separation was regarded as the most complex and key factor to affect the photocatalytic efficiency essentially. Thus, the charge generation and separation properties of the obtained MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials were studied by photo-electrochemical experiments. Firstly, the conversion activity of photons to current was determined via instantaneous photocurrent measurement. Generally, the higher photocurrent signified higher separation efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers, and may bring about higher photocatalytic activity subsequently [56]. As shown in Fig. 8 , the fast photocurrent responses for several on-off cycles can be observed over both the Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 sample. The MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 material displayed higher photocurrent intensity than that of the pure Bi4O5Br2 sample, revealing the introduction of MWCNT to Bi4O5Br2 enabled more efficient separation of photogenerated charge carriers. The in situ strong-coupling between the Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT could result in the sufficient utilization of excellent electron conductivity of MWCNT and sufficient antirecombination of electron-hole pairs photogenerated from Bi4O5Br2 upon light irradiation.

It was generally accepted that most organic compounds can be degraded by photocatalytic oxidation processes, in which involved in a series of photo-generated active species, such as h+, hydroxyl radical (OH), and superoxide radical (O2
−) [57]. To determine the main active species during the photodegradation process, the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique was carried out with DMPO spin-trapping in methanol and water, respectively [58]. As shown in Fig. 11 a, there is no ESR signal for the pure Bi4O5Br2 at dark condition, while the signals of DMPO-O2
− adducts can be observed under the visible light irradiation [59,60]. And the signals of DMPO-OH adducts cannot be found both under dark or light irradiation condition (Fig. 11b). This result indicated that the O2
− may be the main active species for the photo-degradation process, but the OH may be not the main active species. After the MWCNT was introduced to the Bi4O5Br2, the signals of DMPO-O2
− adducts greatly increased (Fig. 11c). Due to the O2
− was produced through the reduction of molecular oxygen via one-electron transfer process, the higher DMPO-O2
− signals of MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 suggested the MWCNT modification could greatly improve the molecular oxygen activation ability. Similar to the Bi4O5Br2, no signals of DMPO-·OH adducts can be seen for the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials, further revealing the ·OH was not the main active species (Fig. 11d).

To gain further insight into the intrinsic reasons that the as-prepared pure Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 can produce O2
− under the visible light irradiation, the XPS valence spectra was employed. In XPS valence spectra, the information on the total density of states (DOS) for the valence band (VB) of semiconductor can obtained. The VB of Bi4O5Br2 were come from the hybridization of the O 2p and Br 4p orbitals, and the conduction band (CB) was mainly consist of Bi 6p states [38]. As shown in Fig. 12 a, both the Bi4O5Br2 and MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 display the VB location at about 1.91eV. According to the DRS analysis, the band gap (Eg) of Bi4O5Br2 was calculated to be 2.19eV (Fig. 12b). Then the CB of the Bi4O5Br2 was estimated to be −0.28eV by using the formula ECB =EVB −Eg. As a result, the O2
− can be yield owning to the E0(O2/O2
−) (−0.046eV vs normal hydrogen electrode (NHE)) was below the CB position (−0.28eV) [38]. With respect to the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2, the introduction of MWCNT boosted the separation of electron-hole pairs and more electrons could activate the molecule oxygen through the one electron reduction. Therefore, more O2
− can be generated via the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials and higher activity can be achieved. And the ·OH can not be generated due to the E0(OH/OH−) was about 2.38eV [61], which was more positive than the VB position. This analysis was consistent with the ESR results.

To further ascertain the active species over MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst, a series of active species trapping experiments were then carried out. As shown in Fig. 13 , after the addition of isopropanol (IPA) to trap OH, no obvious variation of photocatalytic efficiency can be observed, suggesting the OH was not the main active species during the photogradation process. However, the photocatalytic activity significantly decreased after the disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) was added to trap the h+ and O2
−, respectively [62]. Combined with the ESR analysis, the h+ and O2
− were determined to be the main active species for the photocatalysis process.

Fig. 14 illustrates the proposed mechanisms for the increased photocatalytic activity of MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials for the pollutant degradation. After the visible light irradiated on the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials, the electrons in the VB of Bi4O5Br2 could be excited to the CB and the photo-generated holes would leaving in the VB. Subsequently, the photo-generated electrons would transfer from the CB of Bi4O5Br2 to MWCNT due to the excellent electron transfer ability of MWCNT, leading to effective separation of charge carriers. Thereafter, the electrons would reduce the adsorbed molecule oxygen to yield O2
−. The O2
− and photogenerated holes would worked as the main reactive oxygen species for the subsequently photocatalytic degradation reaction.

MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalysts with different MWCNT contents have been controlled prepared via IL assisted solvothermal process. The one-step formation mechanism of the MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials was based on the initial formation of strong coupling between the IL and MWCNT and subsequent resulted in tight junctions between MWCNT and Bi4O5Br2. After the MWCNT was introduced, the photocatalytic activity of MWCNT/Bi4O5Br2 materials increased as compared to the pure Bi4O5Br2 materials. The main active species were determined to be holes and O2
− during the photocatalysis process. This work provided useful information on the design and fabrication of other MWCNT modified semiconductor materials with strong coupling.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21476098, 21471069 and 21576123), and the science and technology support program of Zhenjiang (SH2014018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.molcata.2016.08.029.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
